body_txt: Teaching is more complicated than some people give it credit for; while sometimes a student might struggle to learn things because of a lack of effort or dedication, other times a person finds it difficult to learn something because of how it is taught. The reason why a certain method might not work for someone can include the student having a mental disability, the teacher being incompetent at their job, or the teacher using flawed methods. In these cases, one way to get around it is to teach the student in a way that suits them. There are multiple scenarios in which a student learns better in one way than another. If a student has some kind of condition, such as a mental disorder or a physical disability, then the student will likely learn either by putting them in a special education class or program or by getting them special tutoring. If the method of teaching is flawed, then the student will learn better either by getting a different teacher who can teach them effectively or by having their current teacher adjust their methods. If an otherwise effective method is shown to not be work for someone, the teacher might adjust or tweak the method rather than use an entirely new one. Another variant is to teach a subject in a way that's related to someone's interests or hobbies, such as teaching math to someone who enjoys cooking by presenting math problems in the form of cooking recipes. The trope can apply to schoolwork or to learning skills in general. The former case sometimes appears in stories about special education, to show that people with certain disabilities are capable of learning things if taught in the right way. In stories that criticize the idea of a "one size fits all" learning method, this trope might be used to counter the idea that people who can't learn in standardized ways are simply lazy or unteachable. This can be Played for Drama , especially if the protagonist is a teacher themselves. In such cases, it's often pointed out that offering students an alternate teaching method can work, but if the teacher is trying to teach a large class of 20 to 30 students it just isn't feasible to give each student the attention they need (especially if the class in question is rowdy and they have to spend much of their time keeping the students under control). In addition, even these alternate teaching methods can fail if the student in question has to sit for a rigid, tightly controlled exam. Very common in Save Our Students plots. Someone who's a victim of Limiting the Potential might benefit from this if their old teacher is a Sabotutor . Could involve Autopilot Artistry if someone learns better when doing it subconsciously. Could also be an example of Wax On, Wax Off , if the alternate method involves apparent drudgery. See also Non-Standard Skill Learning , the video game trope for abilities gained through unconventional means.
